cli.usage:
tmoes — GNU/Linux container management tool (modernized C++17 version)

Usage:
  tmoes enter interactive TUI menu
  tmoes [mode] [args...] guides the container to run based on position state machine analysis

Global shortcut command options:
  --lang <zh_CN|en_US> Manually override the internationalized display language
  --quiet Quiet mode (suppress DEBUG and other output)
  --no-color disables color terminal output effects
  --help, -h View this help

docker.readme_content:
service docker start  || systemctl start docker  — start docker
systemctl enable docker  — auto-start docker
---------------------------------
docker ps  — list running containers
docker ps -a  — list all containers
docker start NAME  — start container
docker stop NAME  — stop container
docker attach NAME  — attach to container
docker exec -it NAME /bin/bash  — exec bash in container

welcome: Welcome to tmoes — GNU/Linux container management tool
gui.confirm_x11vnc: Do you need to configure the X11VNC service?
gui.confirm_novnc: Do you need to configure novnc?
gui.x11vnc_configured: After configuring x11vnc, you can enter startx11vnc in the container to start and stopvnc to stop
gui.x11vnc_black_screen_hint: If the screen is black for more than one minute, please enter startx11vnc to restart the service.
gui.vnc.x11vnc_intro: Instructions for starting the X11VNC service:
gui.vnc.x11vnc_details:
There is a difference between x11vnc and tightvnc!
x11vnc can run some applications that tightvnc cannot open, and the experience on WSL2/Linux physical machines is better than tightvnc.
But it may not run as smoothly on Android devices as tightvnc
gui.vnc.recommendation: Recommendation level of vnc server: tiger > x11vnc > tight
gui.vnc.performance_no_accel: Fluency (when accelerated rendering cannot be performed): tight > tiger > x11vnc
gui.vnc.performance_accel: Fluency (when accelerated rendering is supported): tiger > x11vnc ? tight
gui.vnc.configure_multiple: It is recommended that you configure multiple VNC services in case of emergency.

faq.menu_prompt:
What questions do you have?
您有哪些疑问？
faq.q1: software refuses to run as root
faq.q3: fix xfce config file permission denied
faq.q4: cannot connect to settings service / battery info empty
faq.a1:
Creating users on Debian-based systems

Some applications refuse to run as root for security reasons. With great power comes great responsibility. Accidental root operations can damage the system.

You can use the following commands to create a regular user:
# Create a user named moe
adduser moe
# The password is hidden. After creation, you can add the user to the sudo group via this tool.

To elevate privileges, use sudo:
sudo apt update

--------------------

Switching users:
su -       or  sudo su -   or  sudo -i      -> switch to root
su - moe   or  sudo -iu moe               -> switch back to moe
To start VNC as a regular user, first switch to that user, then enter startvnc

--------------------

Arch-based systems:
useradd -m miku     -> create user miku
passwd miku         -> set password
To add to sudo group, use the sudo user group management in Secret Garden -> System

--------------------
faq.a2:
In Android termux chroot (non-proot), running ping 127.0.0.1 as root may cause 'socket Permission denied'.

Solutions:
Method 1:
Enter newgrp aid_inet to switch to the aid_inet group, which has Android network permissions.

Method 2:
Create a regular user, then add them to the sudo group via Secret Garden -> System.
When TMOE_CHROOT=true is detected, the user will automatically be added to aid_inet and aid_net_raw groups.
If your host is Android, these groups are required.

---------------
If some programs cannot access the network, add the user to aid_inet group, e.g. usermod -a -G aid_inet mysql
faq.a3:
chown -Rv ${CURRENT_USER_NAME}:${CURRENT_USER_GROUP} ${HOME}/.config/xfce4
If you encounter other permission issues, you can also run: chown -Rv ${CURRENT_USER_NAME}:${CURRENT_USER_GROUP} ${HOME}
faq.a4:
Cannot connect to settings service — related to dbus. See FAQ VNC/X11 crash option.
Battery monitor issue is also dbus-related.
Note: proot containers may not have permission to start dbus-daemon --system. Skip this.
Only when TMOE_CHROOT=true (tmoe-manager installed chroot/docker), dbus-daemon will auto-start.
If you are not using a tmoe-linux container, manual start is needed.
Start command: sudo dbus-daemon --system --fork
You can also run rm /var/run/dbus/pid to remove the pid file.
faq.a5: Press Enter to uninstall gvfs and udisks2
faq.a6:
If QQ crashes on startup after updating, run rm -rf ~/.config/tencent-qq/ and re-login.
Press Enter to automatically execute the above command
faq.a7:
VNC/X11 crashes are usually dbus-related.
Check if dbus-daemon is properly started.
Start command: dbus-launch --exit-with-session startvnc
Or: export DISPLAY=:1 && dbus-launch startvnc
faq.a8:
If it cannot be opened, manually run rm -rf ~/baidunetdisk
Press Enter to automatically execute rm -vf ~/baidunetdisk/baidunetdiskdata.db
faq.a9: Do you want to uninstall mlocate and catfish?
faq.a10:
There are two simple solutions (っ °Д °)
The former provides a fast terminal emulator that runs on top of the framebuffer; the latter modifies the language variable.
───
Option 1 fbterm: apt install fbterm && fbterm
If startup fails, manually run fbterm
───
Option 2 modify $LANG: export LANG=C.UTF-8
faq.a11:
Do you want to set the hardware clock to local timezone and enable NTP time sync?
timedatectl set-local-rtc 1 --adjust-system-clock
Syncing with Microsoft NTP time server...
ntpdate time.windows.com
timedatectl set-ntp true
If you want to close it, then enter timedatectl set-ntp false
Configuring automatic time sync service...
systemctl enable chrony || systemctl enable chronyd || rc-update add chrony
If you want to disable it, then enter systemctl disable chrony
chronyc sourcestats -v
faq.a11_confirm: Set hardware clock to local timezone and enable NTP?
